<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define electrospinning and list the four key process parameters that control fiber diameter. (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrospinning** is an electrohydrodynamic process in which a polymer solution is drawn into a nano/microfibrous jet by a high-voltage electric field, depositing solid fibers on a grounded collector as a non-woven mat.

**Four Key Parameters Affecting Fiber Diameter:**
1. **Applied Voltage** (1–30 kV) — ↑ voltage initially reduces diameter.
2. **Solution Flow Rate** (0.1–10 mL/hr) — ↑ flow rate increases diameter.
3. **Polymer Concentration** — must be above critical entanglement concentration.
4. **Tip-to-Collector Distance** (5–25 cm) — ↑ distance decreases diameter (more stretching & drying time).

---

### **Q2. What is the role of supercritical CO₂ in gas foaming? Why is this technique preferred for tissue engineering scaffolds? (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q]`

**Model Answer:**

**Role of SC-CO₂:**
- Acts as a **blowing agent** (porogen) that dissolves into the polymer under high pressure and forms **nucleated gas bubbles** upon rapid depressurisation, generating a porous foam scaffold.

**Why Preferred for TE:**
1. **Non-toxic, GRAS (Generally Recognised As Safe)** — no residual solvent in scaffolds → cell-friendly.
2. **Operates at low temperature (31.1 °C)** — prevents thermal degradation of biopolymers.
3. **Adjustable pore size & porosity** by tuning pressure, depressurisation rate, and soaking time.
4. **Environmentally "green"** — eliminates hazardous organic solvents.

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Module 3 Choice Option A
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

#### **(a)** Explain the **Thermally-Induced Phase Separation (TIPS)** process for scaffold fabrication in detail. Discuss the influence of quenching temperature, polymer concentration, and solvent type on scaffold morphology. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** TIPS is a phase-separation technique in which a homogeneous polymer solution is cooled below a critical temperature, inducing **liquid–liquid demixing** into polymer-rich and polymer-lean phases. After solvent removal, the polymer-rich phase forms a porous 3D scaffold.

**Step-by-Step Process (2 marks):**
1. Polymer (e.g., PLLA) dissolved in a solvent (e.g., dioxane) at elevated T.
2. Quenching to sub-zero temperature (−20 °C or liquid N₂).
3. Liquid–liquid demixing via spinodal decomposition or nucleation-growth.
4. Gelation/vitrification of the polymer-rich phase locks pore geometry.
5. Solvent extraction by **freeze-drying (lyophilisation)** yields the final porous scaffold.

**Influence of Process Variables (3 marks):**
| Variable | Effect on Morphology |
|---|---|
| **Quenching temperature (↓)** | Faster demixing, smaller pores, finer nanofibres |
| **Polymer concentration (↑)** | Thicker pore walls, higher mechanical strength, lower porosity |
| **Solvent type (high δ, polar)** | Stronger polymer-solvent interaction, finer fibrous network |

**Typical Morphology:** Nano-fibrous, 50–500 nm fibrils mimicking collagen.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** TIPS yields highly porous, ECM-mimetic scaffolds ideal for bone, nerve, and cartilage regeneration.

---

#### **(b)** Derive the **critical voltage expression** for Taylor cone formation in electrospinning. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Setup (1 mark):** A pendent hemispherical droplet of radius $R$ at the needle tip experiences (i) electrostatic Maxwell stress from the applied field $E$ and (ii) Laplace pressure from surface tension $\gamma$.

**Equations (3 marks):**

$$P_{Laplace} = \dfrac{2\gamma}{R}$$

$$P_{elec} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 E^2}{2}$$

**Equating at threshold (2 marks):**

$$\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 E^2}{2} = \dfrac{2\gamma}{R}$$

With $E = V_c / H$:

$$V_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \gamma H^2}{\varepsilon_0 R}}$$

**Final corrected form with geometric factor (1 mark):**

$$V_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 H^2}{L^2}\left(\ln\dfrac{2L}{R} - 1.5\right)(0.117 \pi \gamma R)}$$

**Conclusion:** Increasing $H$, $\gamma$, and decreasing $R$ raises $V_c$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Module 3 Choice Option B
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

#### **(a)** Describe the **electrospinning setup** and explain the **jet formation mechanism**, including the role of **Taylor cone** and **whipping instability**. Discuss how the fiber diameter is influenced by process parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Setup (1.5 marks):** The electrospinning apparatus consists of four main components:
- A **syringe pump** delivering polymer solution at controlled flow rate.
- A **spinneret (metallic needle)** connected to a **high-voltage DC supply** (1–30 kV).
- A **grounded collector** (stationary plate, rotating drum, or patterned mandrel).
- An **enclosed chamber** to control humidity and temperature.

**Mechanism (3.5 marks):**
1. **Droplet formation:** Polymer solution forms a pendent droplet at the needle tip.
2. **Taylor cone:** Above the critical voltage, surface tension is overcome; the droplet deforms into a cone with half-angle ≈ **49.3°** (Taylor angle).
3. **Jet ejection:** A charged liquid jet is ejected from the cone tip.
4. **Whipping instability:** As the jet travels toward the collector, charge repulsion causes it to undergo chaotic lateral oscillations (bending instability), stretching the jet to nanoscale diameter.
5. **Solidification:** Solvent evaporates mid-flight, depositing solid fibers as a non-woven mat.

**Influence of Parameters (2 marks):**
- **↑ Voltage** → ↓ fiber diameter (until beading occurs).
- **↑ Concentration** → ↑ fiber diameter (above critical entanglement).
- **↑ Flow rate** → ↑ fiber diameter.
- **↑ Conductivity** → ↓ fiber diameter.
- **↑ Distance** → ↓ fiber diameter.

---

#### **(b)** Explain the **gas foaming** technique using **supercritical CO₂**. Derive the **critical nucleus radius** for bubble formation using Classical Nucleation Theory. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Gas Foaming Process (3 marks):**
1. Polymer (e.g., PLGA) is compression-moulded into a disc.
2. Disc is placed in a high-pressure chamber and saturated with **SC-CO₂** (P = 5–30 MPa, T ≈ 35–60 °C). Gas dissolves into the polymer following **Henry's Law** ($C = k_H P$).
3. **Rapid depressurisation** creates a thermodynamic supersaturation.
4. Gas molecules **nucleate** as bubbles within the polymer matrix.
5. Bubbles grow, coalesce, and the matrix vitrifies, locking the porous structure.
6. Final **macroporous foam scaffold** is obtained.

**Advantages (1 mark):** No organic solvents, low temperature, GRAS-grade, environmentally clean.

**Derivation of Critical Nucleus Radius (3 marks):**

Free energy of a spherical gas bubble:

$$\Delta G = -\dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \Delta P + 4\pi r^2 \gamma$$

Setting $\dfrac{d(\Delta G)}{dr} = 0$:

$$-4\pi r^2 \Delta P + 8\pi r \gamma = 0$$

Solving:

$$r^* = \dfrac{2\gamma}{\Delta P}$$

**Conclusion:** Higher pressure differential $\Delta P$ → smaller $r^*$ → finer pores.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to include the **geometric correction factor (0.117 π γ R)** in the critical voltage expression — partial mark deduction (1 mark lost).
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In gas foaming, students **confuse nucleation vs. growth** — remember: nucleation occurs *first*, growth *after* nuclei reach $r^*$.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In TIPS, students often **omit the solvent removal step** (freeze-drying) — full marks require all 5 steps.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing **Henry's Law** (used in gas foaming) with **Laplace's Law** (used in electrospinning Taylor cone) — these are entirely different physics.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Writing "fiber diameter ∝ voltage" without specifying the **range** (true only up to the optimal voltage).

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this list for your KTU 2024 ESE last-day revision!**

- ✅ **Phase Separation (PS)** = thermodynamic demixing of polymer solution into polymer-rich and polymer-lean phases; pores are preserved by freeze-drying.
- ✅ **TIPS** uses **temperature drop**; **NIPS** uses **non-solvent exchange** as triggers for demixing.
- ✅ **Spinodal decomposition** → interconnected pores; **Nucleation-growth** → closed spherical pores.
- ✅ **Electrospinning** uses **electrostatic force** to draw a polymer jet; key components: HV supply, syringe pump, spinneret, collector.
- ✅ **Taylor cone half-angle ≈ 49.3°** appears in every KTU paper.
- ✅ **Critical voltage** $V_c \propto \sqrt{\gamma H^2 / (\varepsilon_0 R)}$ — must include geometric correction in full derivation.
- ✅ **Whipping instability** is the primary mechanism of jet stretching to nanoscale.
- ✅ **Gas foaming** uses **CO₂** as a green blowing agent; **Henry's Law** governs dissolution.
- ✅ **Classical Nucleation Theory** gives $r^* = 2\gamma / \Delta P$ and $\Delta G^* = 16\pi\gamma^3 / 3(\Delta P)^2$.
- ✅ **Supercritical CO₂** conditions: $T_c = 31.1\ °C$, $P_c = 7.38\ MPa$.
- ✅ **Electrospinning** excels at **ECM mimicry** (nano-fibrous); **gas foaming** excels at **macropore creation**; **phase separation** bridges both.
- ✅ **Porosity** of scaffolds: Phase Separation (up to 95%) > Gas Foaming (up to 93%) > Electrospinning mat (40–70%).
- ✅ **Toxic solvent-free** route: **Gas foaming** is the only one of the three.
- ✅ **Final Comparative Edge:** For *bone TE* → use **gas foaming + particulate leaching**; for *neural/cardiac patches* → use **electrospinning**; for *injectable nano-fibrous gels* → use **TIPS**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biofabrication in Tissue Engineering - Biofabrication Strategies - Inkjet Bioprinting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT756 - TISSUE ENGINEERING AND BIOFABRICATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biofabrication in Tissue Engineering - Biofabrication Strategies - Inkjet Bioprinting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Inkjet Bioprinting** is a **non-contact, drop-on-demand (DOD) additive biofabrication technique** that precisely deposits picoliter ($\text{pL}$) to nanoliter ($\text{nL}$) volume droplets of a cell-laden biomaterial (the **bioink**) onto a patterned substrate using digitally controlled thermal, piezoelectric, electrostatic, or acoustic actuation mechanisms. It is classified under the broader umbrella of **jetting-based biofabrication strategies** in KTU 2024 Module 4.

The technique is rooted in conventional 2D inkjet printing but adapted to deposit living cells, growth factors, and biomolecules without compromising their viability. The **resolution** is governed by the **drop diameter** ($d \approx 10$–$100\ \mu\text{m}$), and the **positional accuracy** is typically $\pm 5\ \mu\text{m}$ on a precision $X$–$Y$–$Z$ stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: Inkjet bioprinting is one of the four primary **jetting-based** biofabrication strategies (along with laser-assisted, extrusion, and microvalve bioprinting). The examiner expects students to differentiate **continuous inkjet (CIJ)** vs **drop-on-demand (DOD)** modes.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Gun" of Tissue Engineering

Imagine you are filling a complex coloring book, but instead of crayon shavings, you are firing **tiny, microscopic water-gun droplets** loaded with **living cells** onto a petri dish. Each droplet is fired only when you "demand" it (hence the term **drop-on-demand**). The "gun" mechanism can be:

- A **tiny heater** (thermal inkjet — like a mini kettle that boils a bubble of bioink)
- A **piezo crystal that flexes** when voltage is applied (piezoelectric — like a musical speaker cone that flicks a droplet)
- An **acoustic wave** that pushes the droplet out (acoustic — like a tuning fork vibrating)

Once the droplets land, they **self-assemble**, **crosslink**, and gradually form a 3D tissue-like construct — analogous to how a **3D jigsaw puzzle** is built one tiny piece at a time.

> [!TIP]
> Think of inkjet bioprinting as **3D printing at the resolution of a single cell layer** — it is the closest analog to a desktop printer, but the "ink" is **alive**.

---

## 1.3 Historical Context & Standard Performance Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (KTU Reference) | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Drop volume | $1$ – $100$ | $\text{pL}$ |
| Drop diameter | $10$ – $100$ | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| Nozzle diameter | $20$ – $150$ | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| Printing speed | $1$ – $10{,}000$ | $\text{drops/s}$ |
| Cell density in bioink | $10^6$ – $10^7$ | $\text{cells/mL}$ |
| Post-print cell viability | $\geq 85\%$ | % |
| Positional resolution | $5$ – $50$ | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| Operating temperature (thermal) | $200$ – $300$ | $^\circ\text{C}$ (transient, microseconds) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **transient thermal pulse** in thermal inkjet is **$\sim 2\ \mu\text{s}$** at $200$–$300\ ^\circ\text{C}$, which is short enough to avoid significant thermal damage to cells (only heats $\sim 10\ \text{nL}$ of bioink for microseconds).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Inkjet Bioprinting Strategies

Inkjet bioprinters are classified by **droplet ejection mechanism** and **actuation mode**:

### A. By Actuation Mode

1. **Thermal Inkjet Bioprinting (Bubble Jet)**
   - Uses a **micro-heater resistor** that rapidly vaporizes a thin film of bioink, creating a **nucleated vapor bubble**.
   - The expanding bubble ejects a droplet through the nozzle.
   - **First adapted to biology** by Boland et al. (2003) using a modified HP DeskJet printer.
   - Thermal pulse: $200$–$300\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for $1$–$10\ \mu\text{s}$.

2. **Piezoelectric Inkjet Bioprinting**
   - A **piezoelectric ceramic** (typically lead zirconate titanate, PZT) deforms when a voltage pulse is applied.
   - Deformation creates a pressure wave that ejects a droplet.
   - **No heat involved** — gentler on cells, preferred for sensitive biomolecules.
   - Voltage: $50$ – $200\ \text{V}$; frequency: $1$ – $30\ \text{kHz}$.

3. **Electrostatic / Electrohydrodynamic Inkjet**
   - Uses a strong **electric field** ($10^6$ – $10^7\ \text{V/m}$) between nozzle and substrate.
   - The **Taylor cone** forms and ejects a charged droplet.
   - Suitable for **high-viscosity bioinks** ($> 100\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$).

4. **Acoustic Inkjet**
   - Uses **focused ultrasound** (typically $10$ – $100\ \text{MHz}$) to eject droplets without a nozzle.
   - **Nozzle-less** — eliminates clogging, ideal for highly viscous or cell-dense bioinks.

### B. By Printing Mode

| Mode | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Inkjet (CIJ)** | Continuous stream broken by Rayleigh instability | Industrial marking (rare in bioprinting) |
| **Drop-on-Demand (DOD)** | Droplet ejected only when required | **Standard for bioprinting** |

---

## 2.2 Bioink Formulation Requirements

The bioink must satisfy a tight rheological window for stable droplet formation:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Concept**: The **inverse Ohnesorge number** ($Z$) defines the printable range of bioinks.

$$1 \lt Z \lt 10 \quad \text{where} \quad Z = \frac{1}{Oh} = \frac{\sqrt{\rho \sigma d}}{\mu}$$

- $\rho$ = density of bioink ($\text{kg/m}^3$)
- $\sigma$ = surface tension ($\text{N/m}$)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity ($\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$)
- $d$ = nozzle diameter ($\text{m}$)

**Typical bioink viscosity range**: $1$ – $100\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$ (much lower than extrusion bioprinting which requires $> 10^5\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$).

**Common bioink components**:

- **Hydrogel carriers**: Alginate, gelatin methacrylate (GelMA), collagen, fibrin, hyaluronic acid, PEG
- **Cells**: Fibroblasts, hepatocytes, stem cells (hMSCs, iPSCs), chondrocytes
- **Crosslinkers**: $\text{CaCl}_2$ (for alginate), UV photoinitiator (for GelMA), thrombin (for fibrin)

---

## 2.3 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Droplet Formation

1. **Bioink Loading** — Bioink is loaded into a sterile cartridge reservoir.
2. **Pulse Actuation** — A voltage/thermal pulse is applied to the actuator.
3. **Pressure Wave Generation** — A pressure wave propagates through the bioink.
4. **Meniscus Displacement** — The meniscus at the nozzle tip deforms outward.
5. **Ligament Formation** — A liquid column (ligament) elongates from the nozzle.
6. **Pinch-Off** — Surface tension causes **Rayleigh–Plateau instability**, pinching off a droplet.
7. **Droplet Flight** — The droplet travels through air ($v \approx 1$ – $10\ \text{m/s}$).
8. **Impact & Spreading** — The droplet impacts the substrate, spreading and possibly coalescing.
9. **Crosslinking** — Ionic, thermal, or photopolymerization solidifies the deposited layer.
10. **Layer-by-Layer Buildup** — The $Z$-stage increments, and the next layer is printed.

> [!TIP]
> Each step has a **time constant**: actuation ($\sim \mu\text{s}$), ligament formation ($\sim 100\ \mu\text{s}$), droplet flight ($\sim \text{ms}$), crosslinking ($\sim \text{s}$).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates all KTU 2024 high-yield formulas for Inkjet Bioprinting.** These equations are exam-critical.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Oh = \dfrac{\mu}{\sqrt{\rho \sigma d}}$ | Ohnesorge number (viscous vs inertial vs capillary forces) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $Z = \dfrac{1}{Oh} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\rho \sigma d}}{\mu}$ | Inverse Ohnesorge number — printable window $1 \lt Z \lt 10$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v d}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (inertial/viscous) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $We = \dfrac{\rho v^2 d}{\sigma}$ | Weber number (inertial/surface tension) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $V_{\text{drop}} = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}$ | Volume of a spherical droplet (volume-mode jetting) | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 6 | $V_{\text{jet}} = \pi r^2 L$ | Volume of a cylindrical jet | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{viability}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{post}}}{N_{\text{pre}}} \times 100$ | Cell viability percentage | % |
| 8 | $F_{\text{surface}} = \sigma \cdot L$ | Surface tension force per unit length | $\text{N/m}$ |
| 9 | $P_{\text{jet}} = \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Dynamic (jetting) pressure | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 10 | $E_{\text{kin}} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy of the ejected droplet | $\text{J}$ |
| 11 | $Q = \dfrac{V_{\text{drop}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$ | Volumetric flow rate per nozzle | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 12 | $\Delta t_{\text{thermal}} \approx 2\ \mu\text{s}$ | Thermal pulse duration (thermal inkjet) | $\text{s}$ |
| 13 | $T_{\text{ad}} \approx \dfrac{d^2}{\alpha}$ | Thermal diffusion time scale | $\text{s}$ |
| 14 | $P_{\text{acoustic}} = \rho c v_{\text{particle}}$ | Acoustic radiation pressure | $\text{Pa}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions for stable droplet formation**:
> - $We > We_{\text{crit}} \approx 4$ (sufficient inertia to overcome surface tension)
> - $Re > 10$ (laminar flow)
> - $1 \lt Z \lt 10$ (printable viscosity window)
> - Bioink viscosity: $\mu \le 100\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$
> - Cell density: $\le 10^7\ \text{cells/mL}$ (to prevent sedimentation and clogging)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inkjet bioprinting is commercially used in:

- **Organ-on-chip fabrication** — printing micro-tissues for drug screening (e.g., Organovo's exVive3D™).
- **Skin grafts** — printing layered dermal and epidermal constructs.
- **Bone and cartilage** — printing osteoblast- and chondrocyte-laden scaffolds.
- **Vascularization** — printing sacrificial bioink channels.
- **Personalized medicine** — patient-specific iPSC-laden tissue patches.
- **Pharmaceutical R&D** — high-throughput 3D drug testing platforms.

> [!TIP]
> Companies like **Organovo, CELLINK, 3D Systems, Allevi, and RegenHU** market commercial inkjet-based bioprinters, validating industrial relevance for KTU viva questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Drop Volume from a Cylindrical Jet

**Problem**: A piezoelectric inkjet ejects a cylindrical jet of bioink of length $L = 0.5\ \text{mm}$ through a nozzle of radius $r = 25\ \mu\text{m}$. Calculate the ejected drop volume assuming the entire jet forms a single droplet.

**Step 1: Identify the geometric model.**

The jet is modeled as a cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$. When the jet pinches off due to Rayleigh–Plateau instability, it consolidates into a single spherical droplet (volume conservation assumed).

**Step 2: Express jet volume.**

The volume of a cylinder is:

$$V_{\text{jet}} = \pi r^2 L$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values (with unit consistency).**

Convert to SI units: $r = 25\ \mu\text{m} = 25 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$; $L = 0.5\ \text{mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$.

$$V_{\text{jet}} = \pi \times (25 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})$$

**Step 4: Compute square of radius.**

$$(25 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 625 \times 10^{-12} = 6.25 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 5: Multiply by length.**

$$V_{\text{jet}} = \pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$V_{\text{jet}} = \pi \times 3.125 \times 10^{-13}$$

$$V_{\text{jet}} = 9.817 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 6: Convert to picoliters (pL).**

Since $1\ \text{m}^3 = 10^{15}\ \text{pL}$:

$$V_{\text{jet}} = 9.817 \times 10^{-13} \times 10^{15}\ \text{pL} = 981.7\ \text{pL} \approx 0.98\ \text{nL}$$

**Step 7: Equivalent spherical diameter.**

For a sphere $V = \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$, so $d = \left(\frac{6V}{\pi}\right)^{1/3}$.

$$d = \left(\frac{6 \times 9.817 \times 10^{-13}}{\pi}\right)^{1/3} = (1.875 \times 10^{-12})^{1/3}$$

$$d \approx 1.23 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} = 123\ \mu\text{m}$$

> **Result**: A single inkjet droplet of $\sim 982\ \text{pL}$ corresponds to a sphere of diameter $\sim 123\ \mu\text{m}$ — well within the typical inkjet bioprinting range of $10$–$100\ \mu\text{m}$ for the most common DOD operation, indicating this was a high-volume (low-frequency) jet.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ohnesorge Number for a Collagen Bioink

**Problem**: A collagen bioink has density $\rho = 1010\ \text{kg/m}^3$, surface tension $\sigma = 0.045\ \text{N/m}$, dynamic viscosity $\mu = 12\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s} = 0.012\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. The nozzle diameter is $d = 60\ \mu\text{m} = 60 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$. Determine the printability index $Z$ and classify the bioink as printable, satellite-forming, or non-jetting.

**Step 1: Recall the inverse Ohnesorge number formula.**

$$Z = \frac{\sqrt{\rho \sigma d}}{\mu}$$

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values.**

$$Z = \frac{\sqrt{1010 \times 0.045 \times 60 \times 10^{-6}}}{0.012}$$

**Step 3: Compute the product inside the square root.**

$$1010 \times 0.045 = 45.45$$

$$45.45 \times 60 \times 10^{-6} = 45.45 \times 6 \times 10^{-5} = 272.7 \times 10^{-5} = 2.727 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4: Take the square root.**

$$\sqrt{2.727 \times 10^{-3}} = \sqrt{2.727} \times 10^{-3/2} = 1.6514 \times 0.03162 = 0.05223$$

**Step 5: Divide by viscosity.**

$$Z = \frac{0.05223}{0.012} = 4.353$$

**Step 6: Classify using the empirical window.**

| Range of $Z$ | Behavior |
|---|---|
| $Z \lt 1$ | Viscous dissipation suppresses droplet — **no jetting** |
| $1 \lt Z \lt 10$ | **Stable single-droplet formation — printable** ✅ |
| $Z \gt 10$ | Low viscosity — **satellite droplet formation** |

**Result**: $Z = 4.35$ lies in $1 \lt Z \lt 10$, so the collagen bioink is **printable** and will form **stable single droplets** with no satellite formation. ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cell Viability After Printing

**Problem**: Before thermal inkjet printing, a hepatocyte-laden bioink contains $N_{\text{pre}} = 5.0 \times 10^6$ viable cells per mL. After printing and 24-hour culture, the viable cell count is $N_{\text{post}} = 4.35 \times 10^6$ cells/mL. Calculate cell viability.

**Step 1: Apply the viability formula.**

$$\eta_{\text{viability}} = \frac{N_{\text{post}}}{N_{\text{pre}}} \times 100$$

**Step 2: Substitute.**

$$\eta_{\text{viability}} = \frac{4.35 \times 10^6}{5.0 \times 10^6} \times 100$$

**Step 3: Compute the ratio.**

$$\frac{4.35}{5.0} = 0.87$$

**Step 4: Multiply by 100.**

$$\eta_{\text{viability}} = 0.87 \times 100 = 87\%$$

**Step 5: Compare to KTU benchmark.**

The KTU reference benchmark is $\eta \ge 85\%$ for a successful bioprinting process. Since $87\% > 85\%$, the **print is clinically acceptable** for tissue engineering applications. ✅

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis of Inkjet Subtypes

| Feature | Thermal Inkjet | Piezoelectric Inkjet | Electrostatic Inkjet | Acoustic Inkjet |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Actuator** | Micro-heater | PZT piezo crystal | High-voltage electrode | Focused ultrasound |
| **Voltage/Power** | $\sim 25\ \text{V}$, $\sim 1\ \mu\text{J/drop}$ | $50$–$200\ \text{V}$ | $500$–$3000\ \text{V}$ | $10$–$100\ \text{MHz}$ |
| **Thermal stress** | Transient $200$–$300\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for $\mu\text{s}$ | None | None | Mild |
| **Max bioink viscosity** | $\sim 10\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$ | $\sim 100\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$ | $> 1000\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$ | $> 1000\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| **Cell viability** | $80$–$95\%$ | $85$–$98\%$ | $85$–$95\%$ | $90$–$98\%$ |
| **Resolution** | $\sim 50\ \mu\text{m}$ | $\sim 20$–$50\ \mu\text{m}$ | $\sim 10$–$30\ \mu\text{m}$ | $\sim 10\ \mu\text{m}$ |
| **Clogging risk** | High | High | Low | **None (no nozzle)** |
| **Cost** | Low (modified HP printers) | Medium | Medium-High | High |
| **Commercial example** | HP DeskJet (modified) | JetLab, MicroFab | Custom-built | OptiCell |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight**: The 2024 scheme emphasizes **piezoelectric** inkjet as the **most commonly used** in commercial bioprinters due to its balance of resolution, viability, and absence of thermal stress. Examiners may ask students to justify this preference.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Weber Number Check for Stable Ejection

**Problem**: Verify whether a droplet ejected at $v = 5\ \text{m/s}$ from a $d = 50\ \mu\text{m}$ nozzle with bioink $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and $\sigma = 0.05\ \text{N/m}$ will undergo stable ejection (criterion: $We > We_{\text{crit}} = 4$).

**Step 1: Apply the Weber number formula.**

$$We = \frac{\rho v^2 d}{\sigma}$$

**Step 2: Substitute.**

$$We = \frac{1000 \times (5)^2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{0.05}$$

**Step 3: Compute step-by-step.**

$$v^2 = 25\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\rho v^2 = 1000 \times 25 = 25{,}000\ \text{Pa}$$

$$\rho v^2 d = 25{,}000 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 1.25\ \text{N/m}$$

**Step 4: Divide by surface tension.**

$$We = \frac{1.25}{0.05} = 25$$

**Step 5: Compare to critical value.**

$$We = 25 > We_{\text{crit}} = 4 \quad \checkmark$$

**Result**: Inertia dominates surface tension; **stable droplet ejection is achieved**. However, $We = 25$ is also well above the satellite-formation threshold ($We \approx 50$ for some bioinks), so satellite formation risk is low.

---

## 3.6 Reynolds Number Check (Laminar Flow)

**Step 1: Apply Re formula.**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v d}{\mu}$$

**Step 2: Assume bioink viscosity $\mu = 5\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s} = 0.005\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$.**

$$Re = \frac{1000 \times 5 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{0.005}$$

**Step 3: Compute.**

$$Re = \frac{0.25}{0.005} = 50$$

**Step 4: Check flow regime.**

$Re = 50 < 2300$ (pipe flow transition) → **Laminar flow** ✅. This is desirable because turbulent ejection creates chaotic, non-uniform droplets.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Classification of Inkjet Bioprinting Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INKJET BIOPRINTING]:::main
    A --> B[BY ACTUATION MODE]:::cat
    A --> C[BY PRINTING MODE]:::cat
    B --> D[THERMAL BUBBLE JET]:::sub
    B --> E[PIEZOELECTRIC PZT]:::sub
    B --> F[ELECTROSTATIC EHD]:::sub
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define inkjet bioprinting. List any two advantages and one limitation of the technique.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

**Definition (1 mark):** Inkjet bioprinting is a **non-contact, drop-on-demand (DOD) additive biofabrication technique** that deposits picoliter-scale droplets of cell-laden bioink onto a substrate using digitally controlled thermal, piezoelectric, electrostatic, or acoustic actuation.

**Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
1. **High resolution** — single-cell-level positional accuracy ($5$–$50\ \mu\text{m}$).
2. **High printing speed** — up to $10{,}000$ drops per second per nozzle.
3. **Low cost** — modified commercial inkjet printers (e.g., HP DeskJet) are affordable.
4. **Digital pattern control** — easy CAD-based design without physical masks.
5. **Compatibility with multiple cell types** — supports simultaneous co-culture printing.

**Limitation (1 mark — any one):**
1. **Low-viscosity bioinks only** — viscosity must be $\le 100\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$ to avoid clogging.
2. **Thermal/ mechanical stress on cells** — particularly in thermal and high-frequency piezoelectric modes.
3. **Satellite droplet formation** — leads to poor resolution outside $1 \lt Z \lt 10$.
4. **Limited material range** — only Newtonian or weakly shear-thinning inks are printable.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a bioink? State the four essential rheological properties required for a bioink to be printable by inkjet bioprinting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

**Definition (1 mark):** A **bioink** is a printable biomaterial formulation composed of living cells (and often biomolecules/growth factors) suspended within a biocompatible hydrogel carrier that can be crosslinked post-deposition to form a stable 3D construct.

**Four essential rheological properties (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

| # | Property | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Viscosity** ($\mu$) | $1$ – $100\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| 2 | **Surface tension** ($\sigma$) | $0.03$ – $0.07\ \text{N/m}$ |
| 3 | **Density** ($\rho$) | $\sim 1000$ – $1100\ \text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 4 | **Printability index** ($Z = \sqrt{\rho\sigma d}/\mu$) | $1 \lt Z \lt 10$ |

> Additional desirable properties: shear-thinning behaviour, non-Newtonian stability under jetting shear rates ($> 10^4\ \text{s}^{-1}$), and biocompatibility post-crosslinking.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **working principle of a piezoelectric inkjet bioprinter** with a neat block diagram. Explain how droplet ejection is achieved without thermal stress on cells. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A collagen-based bioink has the following properties: $\rho = 1020\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\sigma = 0.048\ \text{N/m}$, $\mu = 8\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$. The nozzle diameter is $d = 50\ \mu\text{m}$, and the droplet ejection velocity is $v = 4\ \text{m/s}$. Determine:
  (i) the **inverse Ohnesoge number** $Z$ and classify printability,
  (ii) the **Weber number** $We$ and check whether $We > We_{\text{crit}} = 4$,
  (iii) the **Reynolds number** $Re$ and comment on the flow regime. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Piezoelectric Inkjet (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Definition & context:**

A **piezoelectric inkjet bioprinter** uses the converse piezoelectric effect — when a voltage is applied to a piezoelectric ceramic (typically **lead zirconate titanate, PZT**), the crystal deforms mechanically, generating a pressure pulse that ejects a droplet of bioink.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Component description (refer to Diagram 4.3):**

The system comprises:
- A **voltage pulse generator** (typically $50$–$200\ \text{V}$).
- A **piezoelectric crystal (PZT)** bonded to a glass capillary or chamber.
- A **bioink reservoir** and a **micro-nozzle** ($20$–$150\ \mu\text{m}$ diameter).
- An **$X$–$Y$–$Z$ motorized stage** synchronized with droplet ejection.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Droplet ejection sequence:**

1. A voltage pulse ($1$ – $30\ \text{kHz}$) is applied to the PZT.
2. The PZT deforms inward, compressing the bioink → pressure wave.
3. Pressure wave propagates to the nozzle tip → meniscus bulges outward.
4. A liquid **ligament** elongates and pinches off due to **Rayleigh–Plateau instability** → forms a single droplet.
5. Droplet travels to substrate, spreads, and crosslinks.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Why no thermal stress:**

Unlike thermal inkjet (where a heater vaporizes the bioink at $200$–$300\ ^\circ\text{C}$), the piezoelectric mechanism relies purely on **mechanical deformation**. **No heat is generated**, so heat-sensitive biomolecules (growth factors, DNA, RNA, proteins) and mammalian cells remain viable ($>$ 95% post-print viability typical).

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Distinguishing feature:**

The **absence of thermal actuation** makes piezoelectric inkjet suitable for **gentle biomolecule printing**, including protein microarrays and DNA biosensors, in addition to tissue engineering.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**

$$\rho = 1020\ \text{kg/m}^3,\quad \sigma = 0.048\ \text{N/m},\quad \mu = 8\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s} = 0.008\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

$$d = 50\ \mu\text{m} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m},\quad v = 4\ \text{m/s}$$

---

**Part (i) — Inverse Ohnesorge number $Z$ (2 marks):**

The formula is:

$$Z = \frac{\sqrt{\rho \sigma d}}{\mu}$$

Substituting:

$$Z = \frac{\sqrt{1020 \times 0.048 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}}{0.008}$$

Computing the product inside the square root:

$$1020 \times 0.048 = 48.96$$

$$48.96 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 2448 \times 10^{-6} = 2.448 \times 10^{-3}$$

Taking the square root:

$$\sqrt{2.448 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.04948$$

Dividing by viscosity:

$$Z = \frac{0.04948}{0.008} = 6.185$$

**Classification (1 mark):** Since $1 \lt Z = 6.185 \lt 10$, the bioink is **printable** and forms **stable single droplets** without satellites. ✅

---

**Part (ii) — Weber number (2 marks):**

$$We = \frac{\rho v^2 d}{\sigma}$$

$$We = \frac{1020 \times 16 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{0.048}$$

Numerator: $1020 \times 16 = 16320$; $16320 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 0.816\ \text{N/m}$.

$$We = \frac{0.816}{0.048} = 17.0$$

**Check (1 mark):** $We = 17.0 > We_{\text{crit}} = 4$ → **Stable droplet ejection is achieved** (inertia overcomes surface tension). ✅

---

**Part (iii) — Reynolds number (2 marks):**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v d}{\mu}$$

$$Re = \frac{1020 \times 4 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{0.008}$$

Numerator: $1020 \times 4 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 0.204$.

$$Re = \frac{0.204}{0.008} = 25.5$$

**Flow regime comment (1 mark):** $Re = 25.5 \ll 2300$ → flow is **deeply laminar** ✅. This is favourable — turbulent ejection would create non-uniform, chaotic droplets.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare **thermal inkjet** and **piezoelectric inkjet** bioprinting in terms of actuation mechanism, bioink compatibility, cell viability, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Rayleigh–Plateau instability** and its role in **drop-on-demand (DOD) droplet formation** in inkjet bioprinting. Describe the four sequential stages from meniscus deformation to droplet impact. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks)

**Comparison table (5 marks — 1 mark per row + 1 mark for overall synthesis):**

| Feature | Thermal Inkjet | Piezoelectric Inkjet |
|---|---|---|
| **Actuation** | Micro-heater vaporizes bioink → bubble ejection | PZT crystal mechanically deforms → pressure wave |
| **Thermal stress** | Transient $200$–$300\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $1$–$10\ \mu\text{s}$ | None — purely mechanical |
| **Max bioink viscosity** | $\sim 10\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$ | $\sim 100\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| **Cell viability** | $80$–$95\%$ | $85$–$98\%$ |
| **Resolution** | $\sim 50\ \mu\text{m}$ | $\sim 20$–$50\ \mu\text{m}$ |
| **Cost** | Low (modified HP inkjet cartridges) | Medium (JetLab, MicroFab) |
| **Applications** | Tissue patches, skin, cartilage | Protein/DNA microarrays, sensitive cells |

**Synthesis (2 marks):**
- **Thermal inkjet** is the **low-cost entry point** for tissue engineering, but its transient heat pulse can denature heat-sensitive growth factors (e.g., BMP-2, TGF-β).
- **Piezoelectric inkjet** is the **industry-preferred** mode in 2024 commercial bioprinters because it achieves higher resolution, broader bioink compatibility, and superior cell viability.
- **KTU recommendation**: For exam questions requiring a single best technique, choose **piezoelectric** for biological applications, **thermal** for cost-limited prototyping.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Rayleigh–Plateau Instability (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Physical principle:**

The **Rayleigh–Plateau instability** is a capillary-driven phenomenon: a **cylindrical liquid column is inherently unstable** when its length exceeds its circumference ($L > 2\pi r$). Surface tension drives the column to break up into discrete spherical droplets, because a sphere has the **minimum surface area for a given volume**, minimizing surface energy.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Mathematical criterion:**

The most unstable wavelength is $\lambda_{\text{dom}} \approx 9.02 \cdot r$ (Rayleigh's result). Drop diameter after breakup:

$$d_{\text{drop}} \approx 1.89 \cdot d_{\text{nozzle}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Why it matters in DOD:**

In DOD, the ejected liquid jet from the nozzle is a cylinder. Surface tension at the jet surface is the restoring force; inertia is the destabilizing force. The dimensionless competition is captured by the **Weber number** $We = \rho v^2 d / \sigma$. When $We$ exceeds a critical value, the jet pinches off to form a single, well-defined droplet.

**Step 4 (3 marks) — Four sequential stages (refer to Diagram 4.5):**

1. **Meniscus Deformation** — Pressure pulse pushes the nozzle meniscus outward by $1$–$5\ \mu\text{m}$.
2. **Ligament Elongation** — A liquid column (ligament) of length $L > 2\pi r$ extends from the nozzle.
3. **Pinch-Off (Satellite suppression)** — Rayleigh–Plateau instability causes a single pinch-off at $\lambda_{\text{dom}} \approx 9.02 r$, forming one main droplet (no satellites if $1 \lt Z \lt 10$).
4. **Droplet Impact & Spreading** — Droplet travels at $v = 1$–$10\ \text{m/s}$, impacts the substrate, spreads to a footprint of $d_{\text{spread}} \approx 2$–$3 \times d_{\text{drop}}$, and coalesces with neighbouring droplets before crosslinking.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Inkjet Bioprinting Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Thermal vs Piezoelectric mechanism** (–2 marks): Many students write "thermal inkjet uses piezo crystal" — these are **mutually exclusive** actuation modes. Memorize: Thermal = heat; Piezo = mechanical deformation.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in $Z$, $We$, $Re$ calculations** (–1.5 marks): All three must be dimensionless. Convert $\mu\text{m}$ to m, $\text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$ to $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ before substitution.
>
> 3. **Mixing up Ohnesorge vs Weber vs Reynolds definitions** (–2 marks):
>    - $Oh$ → viscous vs capillary (no velocity)
>    - $We$ → inertial vs surface tension (no viscosity)
>    - $Re$ → inertial vs viscous (no surface tension)
>
> 4. **Not stating the bioink viscosity range** (–1 mark): KTU explicitly requires $\mu \le 100\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$ for inkjet. Writing "low viscosity" without a number loses marks.
>
> 5. **Omitting cell viability** in advantages (–0.5 marks): Always quantify (e.g., "85–98%").
>
> 6. **Not drawing a labelled diagram** for the 14-mark question (–2 marks): A schematic is **mandatory** for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist for KTU 2024 — Inkjet Bioprinting**

### Core Concepts
- Inkjet bioprinting is a **non-contact, drop-on-demand (DOD)** technique that deposits picoliter droplets of bioink.
- It is one of the four **jetting-based** biofabrication strategies (Module 4).
- Resolution: $10$–$100\ \mu\text{m}$; drop volume: $1$–$100\ \text{pL}$.

### Four Actuation Modes
- **Thermal (bubble jet)**: micro-heater at $200$–$300\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for $\mu\text{s}$; uses modified HP DeskJet printers.
- **Piezoelectric (PZT)**: voltage-induced mechanical deformation; **no heat**; preferred for cells.
- **Electrostatic (EHD)**: high-voltage Taylor cone; high-viscosity bioinks.
- **Acoustic (nozzle-less)**: focused ultrasound; no clogging.

### Bioink Requirements
- Viscosity: $1$ – $100\ \text{mPa}\cdot\text{s}$ (Newtonian or weakly shear-thinning).
- Surface tension: $0.03$ – $0.07\ \text{N/m}$.
- Cell density: $\le 10^7\ \text{cells/mL}$.
- Must be **printable**: $1 \lt Z \lt 10$.

### Key Formulas (must memorize)
- $Oh = \mu / \sqrt{\rho \sigma d}$
- $Z = 1/Oh = \sqrt{\rho \sigma d} / \mu$
- $We = \rho v^2 d / \sigma$ — criterion: $We > 4$
- $Re = \rho v d / \mu$ — criterion: $Re < 2300$ (laminar)
- $V_{\text{drop}} = \pi d^3 / 6$ (sphere)
- $\eta_{\text{viability}} = N_{\text{post}}/N_{\text{pre}} \times 100\%$

### Critical Process Parameters
- **Printable window**: $1 \lt Z \lt 10$
- **Stable ejection**: $We > 4$
- **Laminar jetting**: $Re \ll 2300$
- **Cell viability benchmark**: $\ge 85\%$ post-print
- **Thermal pulse (thermal inkjet)**: $\sim 2\ \mu\text{s}$ at $200$–$300\ ^\circ\text{C}$ — short enough to spare cells

### Advantages
- High resolution, high speed (up to $10^4$ drops/s/nozzle), low cost, digital CAD control, multi-cell-type co-culture.

### Limitations
- Low-viscosity bioinks only, risk of nozzle clogging, satellite droplets outside $Z$ window, cell stress in thermal mode.

### Applications
- Skin grafts, bone/cartilage, vascular channels, organ-on-chip, drug screening microarrays, protein/DNA biosensors.

### Commercial Examples (viva-favourite)
- **Organovo** (exVive3D™), **CELLINK**, **Allevi**, **RegenHU**, **3D Systems**, **JetLab**, **MicroFab**.

### Common Exam Questions
1. Compare thermal vs piezoelectric inkjet (7 marks).
2. Define $Z$, derive the printable window (3 marks).
3. Numerical: calculate $Z$, $We$, $Re$ and comment on printability (7 marks).
4. Draw a labelled schematic of the working of an inkjet bioprinter (7 marks).
5. List the rheological requirements of a bioink (3 marks).

> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip**: Always end 14-mark answers with **one industrial application** (e.g., Organovo's liver tissue) and **one quantitative benchmark** (e.g., $\eta \ge 85\%$). This signals depth and earns the last 1–2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT756 - TISSUE ENGINEERING AND BIOFABRICATION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Laser-Assisted Bioprinting - Extrusion Bioprinting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Laser-Assisted Bioprinting (LAB)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Laser-Assisted Bioprinting (LAB)* is a **digitally controlled, nozzle-free** additive biofabrication technique that uses a focused pulsed laser beam to transfer picolitre-to-nanolitre volumes of bioink from a donor "ribbon" onto a receiving substrate, where the cells are deposited in a predefined 2D/3D pattern. The most studied variant is **Laser-Induced Forward Transfer (LIFT)**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnifying Glass Punch"
Imagine aiming a magnifying glass at bright sunlight until the focal spot is so intense that it **burns a tiny crater into a leaf**. Now replace the leaf with a thin film of biological "ink" sitting on a glass slide, and replace the sun with a precisely pulsed laser. The laser's focal spot instantly vaporises a micro-region of an **energy-absorbing layer**, creating a micro-bubble that *punches* a tiny droplet of bioink downward onto a collector placed below — much like a **non-contact, high-speed dot-matrix printer for living cells**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key operational metrics (industry standard for LAB):**
> - **Printing resolution:** 5 – 50 µm (single-droplet diameter)
> - **Cell viability:** typically > 95 %
> - **Bioink viscosity range:** 1 – 300 mPa·s
> - **Compatible cell densities:** up to ~10⁸ cells/mL
> - **Droplet volume:** 0.1 – 10 nL per pulse

---

## 1.2 Extrusion Bioprinting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Extrusion Bioprinting* is a **continuous-filament deposition** technique in which a viscoelastic bioink (hydrogel, paste, or melt) is dispensed under pneumatic, mechanical-piston, or screw-driven pressure through a nozzle to fabricate 3D constructs layer-by-layer on a computer-controlled XYZ stage.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Cake Icing Bag
Hold a piping bag filled with coloured icing, squeeze the bag, and a **continuous ribbon of icing** flows out the nozzle to draw shapes on a cake. The nozzle moves in **X–Y**, lays down a pattern, the cake is lowered by one **layer thickness (Z)**, and the next layer is drawn on top. Substitute icing with **cell-laden hydrogel** (e.g., alginate, GelMA, Pluronic), the cake with a **sterile petri dish / build platform**, and the icing bag with a **syringe + actuator** — that is extrusion bioprinting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key operational metrics (industry standard for Extrusion):**
> - **Printing resolution:** 100 – 500 µm (line width)
> - **Cell viability:** 40 – 95 % (viscosity- and shear-dependent)
> - **Bioink viscosity range:** 30 – 6 × 10⁷ mPa·s (six orders of magnitude!)
> - **Compatible cell densities:** high (cell aggregates / bio-paste)
> - **Throughput:** highest among all bioprinting modalities

---

## 1.3 Where the Two Sit in the Bioprinting Family

| Modality | Resolution (µm) | Cell Viability (%) | Viscosity (mPa·s) | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Laser-Assisted (LAB) | **5 – 50** | > 95 | 1 – 300 | High |
| Inkjet (Drop-on-Demand) | 50 – 200 | 85 – 95 | 3 – 30 | Medium |
| **Extrusion** | 100 – 500 | 40 – 95 | 30 – 6 × 10⁷ | Low – Medium |
| Stereolithography (SLA) | 25 – 100 | 85 – 95 | 1 – 5000 (resin) | Medium – High |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Laser-Assisted Bioprinting — Operational Logic

**Why "nozzle-free"?** Because the laser beam is *focused*, not extruded. There is no needle, no contact, and therefore no shear-induced cell damage.

**How does the transfer happen?** Five sequential steps:

1. **Ribbon preparation** — A glass/quartz slide is spin-coated with:
   - (a) an **energy-absorbing layer** (e.g., gold, titanium, graphene, or polymer) of 10 – 100 nm thickness, and
   - (b) a thin **bioink film** (typically 30 – 100 µm thick) coated on top.
2. **Laser pulse generation** — A pulsed solid-state laser (Nd:YAG @ 1064 nm, or UV excimer) emits nanosecond-to-femtosecond pulses (typical: 5 – 10 ns, 1 – 100 µJ energy).
3. **Focused irradiation** — A microscope objective or scanner (galvanometer) focuses the pulse onto a ~5 – 50 µm spot on the ribbon's absorbing layer.
4. **Vapour-bubble formation** — The absorbing layer locally vaporises in < 1 µs, generating a high-pressure cavitation bubble (peak pressure ~ 100 MPa) that **propels a jet of bioink** toward the receiving substrate.
5. **Droplet landing & crosslinking** — The droplet lands on the collector; subsequent photopolymerisation, ionic, or thermal gelation fixes it in place.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical process parameters** in LAB:
> - **Laser fluence (F)** — energy per unit area (J/cm²). Must exceed a *threshold fluence* $F_{th}$ for droplet ejection.
> - **Spot diameter (d)** — sets minimum feature size.
> - **Ribbon–substrate gap (h)** — typically 0.5 – 2 mm; controls droplet flight and trajectory.
> - **Pulse duration (τ)** — nanosecond pulses balance cell viability and transfer efficiency.
> - **Bioink rheology** — surface tension, viscosity determine jet stability.

---

## 2.2 Extrusion Bioprinting — Operational Logic

**Why is it the most popular?** Because it can print **highly viscous and cell-dense materials** (including spheroids and decellularised ECM pastes) that no other modality can handle.

**How does it work?** Four sequential steps:

1. **Bioink loading** — Hydrogel precursor (e.g., alginate, GelMA, collagen, Pluronic F-127) mixed with cells is loaded into a **syringe / barrel**.
2. **Actuation** — One of three mechanisms drives the plunger / screw:
   - **Pneumatic** — compressed air/gas pressure (typical 0.1 – 5 bar)
   - **Mechanical piston** — stepper motor drives a plunger (high positional control)
   - **Screw-driven (auger)** — rotating screw generates force (best for very high viscosity pastes)
3. **Filament extrusion** — The bioink flows through a **cylindrical nozzle** (typical inner diameter 100 – 800 µm).
4. **Layer-by-layer deposition** — The nozzle is rastered in X–Y; the build platform drops in Z; the next layer is dispensed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical process parameters** in Extrusion:
> - **Nozzle inner diameter (D)** — sets line width.
> - **Applied pressure / flow rate (Q)** — sets filament velocity.
> - **Print speed (v)** — must match flow rate to avoid over/under-extrusion.
> - **Bioink viscosity (µ)** and **yield stress (τ_y)** — must be high enough to retain shape.
> - **Wall shear stress (τ_w)** inside the nozzle — must be < ~5 kPa to maintain cell viability.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = \dfrac{E_p}{A_{spot}}$ | Laser fluence = pulse energy / spot area | J/cm² | LAB droplet threshold |
| 2 | $v_{avg} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{cs}} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Average bioink velocity in nozzle | m/s | Extrusion flow |
| 3 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \, \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille volumetric flow | m³/s | Newtonian bioink in cylindrical nozzle |
| 4 | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}$ | Wall shear stress in nozzle | Pa | Cell viability check |
| 5 | $u(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\,(R^2 - r^2)$ | Parabolic velocity profile | m/s | Velocity distribution |
| 6 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v_{avg} \, D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (laminar < 2100) | dimensionless | Flow regime check |
| 7 | $Oh = \dfrac{\mu}{\sqrt{\rho \,\sigma \, D}}$ | Ohnesorge number (printability) | dimensionless | Droplet stability |
| 8 | $We = \dfrac{\rho \, v^2 \, D}{\sigma}$ | Weber number (inertia / surface tension) | dimensionless | Droplet ejection |
| 9 | $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^3}$ | Wall shear rate in nozzle | s⁻¹ | Shear exposure time |
| 10 | $P_{print} = \dfrac{32 \mu L v_{avg}}{D^2}$ | Printing pressure required (Newtonian) | Pa | Pneumatic design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's rule of thumb** for extrusion:
> *If $Re \le 2100$ the flow is laminar (good); if $Re \gg 2100$ the flow is turbulent (bad — cells lyse).* Keep $\tau_w < 5$ kPa to preserve > 80 % cell viability for most mammalian cell lines.

### Real-World Utility
- **LAB** is used commercially for skin grafts (Poietis — now merged with 3D Systems), corneal tissue, and high-precision heterogeneous cell patterning.
- **Extrusion** dominates the market for bone/cartilage scaffolds (Organovo, Cellink, Allevi), 3D-printed meat (Aleph Farms), and large-volume organoid fabrication.

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption Block (used throughout):**
> The bioink is treated as a **Newtonian fluid** of constant viscosity $\mu$ and density $\rho$. The flow is **steady, incompressible, laminar, fully-developed, axisymmetric, and isothermal**, with **no body forces** and **no-slip** at the nozzle wall.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Velocity Profile of a Newtonian Bioink in a Cylindrical Nozzle

### Step 1 — Governing equation
For a fully-developed, axisymmetric flow in the z-direction, the z-component of the Navier–Stokes equation reduces to:

$$
0 \;=\; -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial z} \;+\; \mu\!\left[\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\,\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial r}\right)\right]
$$

*Logic:* The convective and unsteady terms vanish because the flow is fully-developed and steady; only the axial pressure gradient balances viscous diffusion.

### Step 2 — Apply constant pressure gradient
For a constant $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial z} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$, where $\Delta P$ is the pressure drop across nozzle length $L$:

$$
\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,\dfrac{du}{dr}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu L}
$$

### Step 3 — First integration (multiply by r and integrate over r)

$$
r\,\dfrac{du}{dr} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L}\,r^{2} \;+\; C_{1}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply boundary condition at the centreline (r = 0)
The velocity gradient must be **finite** at the centreline (no singularities): $\left.\dfrac{du}{dr}\right|_{r=0} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_{1} = 0$.

$$
\dfrac{du}{dr} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L}\,r
$$

### Step 5 — Second integration (integrate over r)

$$
u(r) \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\,r^{2} \;+\; C_{2}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply no-slip boundary condition at the wall (r = R)
At the nozzle wall, the bioink velocity equals the wall velocity (zero for a fixed nozzle): $u(R) = 0$.

$$
0 \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\,R^{2} \;+\; C_{2}
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
C_{2} \;=\; -\dfrac{\Delta P\,R^{2}}{4 \mu L}
$$

### Step 7 — Final parabolic velocity profile

$$
\boxed{\,u(r) \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\,\bigl(R^{2} - r^{2}\bigr)\,}
$$

*Logic:* This is a **parabola opening downward**, with the maximum at the centreline $r=0$ and zero at the wall $r=R$.

### Step 8 — Maximum (centreline) velocity

$$
u_{max} \;=\; u(0) \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P\,R^{2}}{4 \mu L}
$$

### Step 9 — Average velocity (used in flow-rate and Reynolds-number calculations)
By symmetry, average velocity is half the maximum for parabolic flow:

$$
v_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{u_{max}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P\,R^{2}}{8 \mu L}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Volumetric Flow Rate (Hagen–Poiseuille Law)

### Step 1 — Define flow rate as the integral of local velocity over the nozzle cross-section

$$
Q \;=\; \int_{0}^{R} u(r)\cdot 2\pi r\, dr
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the velocity profile from Section 3.1, Step 7

$$
Q \;=\; \int_{0}^{R} \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\,(R^{2} - r^{2})\cdot 2\pi r\, dr
$$

### Step 3 — Pull out constants and expand

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{0}^{R} \bigl(R^{2} r - r^{3}\bigr)\, dr
$$

### Step 4 — Perform the integration

$$
\int_{0}^{R} R^{2} r\, dr \;=\; \dfrac{R^{4}}{2}, \qquad
\int_{0}^{R} r^{3}\, dr \;=\; \dfrac{R^{4}}{4}
$$

$$
Q \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L}\!\left(\dfrac{R^{4}}{2} - \dfrac{R^{4}}{4}\right)
\;=\; \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L}\!\left(\dfrac{R^{4}}{4}\right)
$$

### Step 5 — Final Hagen–Poiseuille result

$$
\boxed{\,Q \;=\; \dfrac{\pi R^{4}\,\Delta P}{8 \mu L}\,}
$$

*Logic:* Flow rate scales with the **fourth power of the nozzle radius** — halving the nozzle diameter cuts flow 16-fold. This is why extrusion bioprinters struggle with sub-200 µm nozzles for high-viscosity bioinks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Wall Shear Stress

### Step 1 — Newtonian constitutive relation
For a Newtonian fluid, shear stress is proportional to the local velocity gradient:

$$
\tau_{rz}(r) \;=\; \mu\,\dfrac{du}{dr}
$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate the velocity profile (from Section 3.1, Step 4)

$$
\dfrac{du}{dr} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L}\,r
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$
\tau_{rz}(r) \;=\; \mu \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L}\,r \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P\,r}{2 L}
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate at the nozzle wall (r = R)

$$
\boxed{\,\tau_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 L}\,}
$$

*Logic:* Wall shear stress grows linearly with nozzle radius and pressure drop. For typical 200 µm (R = 100 µm) nozzles printing alginate at $\mu = 100$ mPa·s, a flow rate of 1 µL/s produces $\tau_w \approx 0.5$ kPa — well within the safe range.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Reynolds Number for Printing-Flow Classification

### Step 1 — Use characteristic length = nozzle diameter $D = 2R$ and characteristic velocity = $v_{avg}$

$$
Re \;=\; \dfrac{\rho\,v_{avg}\,D}{\mu}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $v_{avg} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi R^{2}}$

$$
Re \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \cdot \dfrac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} \cdot 2R}{\mu} \;=\; \dfrac{2 \rho Q}{\pi R \mu}
$$

*Logic:* For a 2 % alginate solution ($\rho \approx 1010$ kg/m³, $\mu \approx 0.5$ Pa·s) flowing at 1 µL/s through a 200 µm nozzle, $Re \approx 6.4 \times 10^{-3}$ — **deeply laminar**, exactly what we want for cell printing.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Laser Fluence Threshold for Droplet Ejection

### Step 1 — Define fluence
Fluence is the laser pulse energy $E_p$ distributed over the Gaussian spot of radius $w$:

$$
F \;=\; \dfrac{E_{p}}{\pi w^{2}}
$$

### Step 2 — Threshold condition
Droplet ejection requires $F \ge F_{th}$, where $F_{th}$ is the energy density just sufficient to vaporise a micro-volume of the absorbing layer. Empirically:

$$
F_{th} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_{L}\,L_{v}\,d_{ink}}{\eta_{abs}\,\eta_{th}}
$$

where
- $\rho_{L}$ = density of bioink liquid,
- $L_{v}$ = latent heat of vaporisation of the absorbing layer (for gold: $L_v \approx 1.65 \times 10^6$ J/kg),
- $d_{ink}$ = effective thickness of the bioink layer,
- $\eta_{abs}$ = absorption efficiency of the layer at the laser wavelength,
- $\eta_{th}$ = thermo-mechanical conversion efficiency (typically 0.05 – 0.3).

### Step 3 — Operating window
**Sub-threshold ($F < F_{th}$):** no transfer.
**Near-threshold ($F \approx F_{th}$):** sub-picolitre droplets, highest cell viability.
**Super-threshold ($F \gg F_{th}$):** jets, splashing, photothermal damage — kills cells.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Process-Parameter Comparison

| Parameter | Laser-Assisted Bioprinting (LAB) | Extrusion Bioprinting |
|---|---|---|
| **Driving force** | Photothermal vapour bubble | Pneumatic / piston / screw |
| **Nozzle?** | None (nozzle-free) | Yes (mandatory) |
| **Resolution control** | Laser spot diameter (5 – 50 µm) | Nozzle diameter (100 – 800 µm) |
| **Cell-stress source** | Photothermal / shockwave | Wall shear stress $\tau_w$ |
| **Governing equation** | $F = E_p / \pi w^2$ | Hagen–Poiseuille $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta P / 8\mu L$ |
| **Printable viscosity** | 1 – 300 mPa·s | 30 – 6×10⁷ mPa·s |
| **Cell viability** | > 95 % | 40 – 95 % |
| **Throughput** | Low (serial droplet) | High (continuous filament) |
| **Scalability** | Poor (research-scale) | Excellent (industrial-scale) |
| **Cost** | High (laser + optics) | Low (3-axis stage) |
| **Best for** | Heterogeneous micro-patterning, high cell viability | High-density tissues, bone/cartilage, organoids |
